questions.prompt: Why is Tesla pursuing humanoid robots instead of specialized industrial robots for the tasks current automation can't handle?

choices.text: Factories (and homes, warehouses, businesses) are already designed for humans. A humanoid robot adapts to those environments without redesigning them. Specialized robots require environment redesign per task. The bet: a single general humanoid replaces many specialized solutions AND addresses much larger markets beyond factories. Trade-off: humanoid form is harder to engineer (balance, manipulation, perception) but unlocks more potential use cases.

questions.explanation: Humanoid form lets robots work in environments designed for humans without redesigning those environments. Specialized robots win on narrow tasks; humanoids — IF they work — win on breadth. Tesla's bet is breadth. The technical challenge is real.

questions.prompt: A manufacturing leader asks: 'Should we plan our 2028 factory assuming Optimus replaces line workers?' Right answer?

choices.text: No. Optimus is in early factory deployment for limited tasks as of 2026. Realistic factory-scale deployment for non-trivial tasks is 2028-2032 range, with significant uncertainty. Build today's factories assuming current hybrid human-robot reality; fold in humanoid robots as they prove out at scale. Don't bet factory production on a technology that hasn't fully shipped yet.

questions.explanation: Optimus is real but early. Production-scale deployment is years out with uncertain timeline. Build for current reality; adapt as humanoid robots prove out. Don't risk factory operations on optimistic R&D timelines.
